title: Concrete retaining wall cost per linear foot in 2026
excerpt: A concrete retaining wall costs $30-$95 per linear foot in 2026 — typically $40-$75 per foot at 3-4 feet tall, with engineered walls over 4 feet pushing higher.
metaKeywords: retaining wall cost per linear foot
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed retaining wall, foundation, slab, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a poured concrete retaining wall runs $30-$95 per linear foot installed nationally, with $40-$75 per foot the most common quote band at 3-4 feet tall. Concrete masonry unit (CMU) block walls run $25-$70 per linear foot at the same height. Wall height is the single largest cost driver: per the International Code Council IRC R404, walls over 4 feet tall require engineered design and footings sized to the soil class, which roughly doubles the per-foot cost vs unengineered 3-foot walls. Drainage system (perforated drain tile plus gravel backfill), wall reinforcement (vertical rebar tied to footing), and excavation depth account for most of the remaining variation between low and high quotes.

faqs.question: How much does a concrete retaining wall cost per linear foot in 2026?
faqs.answer: A poured concrete retaining wall runs $30-$95 per linear foot in 2026, with $40-$75 per foot the most common band at 3-4 feet tall. CMU block walls at the same height run $25-$70 per linear foot. Walls under 3 feet tall price toward the low end; engineered walls over 4 feet jump to $75-$150+ per foot because of structural design, deeper footing, and heavier reinforcement requirements.

faqs.question: What height retaining wall requires an engineered design?
faqs.answer: Per the International Code Council IRC R404 (residential code), retaining walls over 4 feet tall measured from the bottom of the footing to the top of the wall require an engineered design stamped by a licensed structural engineer. Some jurisdictions trigger the engineering requirement at 3 feet for walls supporting a surcharge load (driveway, structure, vehicle). Engineering fees add $500-$2,500 to project cost.

faqs.question: Is poured concrete cheaper than block for a retaining wall?
faqs.answer: Block (CMU) is typically 15-30 percent cheaper than poured concrete on a wall-only basis at residential heights. Poured concrete wins on speed of installation (1-2 days vs 3-5 days for block), water resistance (no mortar joints to fail), and structural integrity for taller walls. Most engineered walls over 6 feet are poured concrete; most short cosmetic walls are block or segmental retaining wall (SRW) units.

faqs.question: Do retaining walls need drainage?
faqs.answer: Yes — drainage is the single highest-leverage line item in retaining wall design. A perforated drain tile in a gravel envelope at the back of the footing, draining to daylight or a sump, prevents hydrostatic pressure buildup behind the wall. The Federal Highway Administration cites inadequate drainage as the leading cause of retaining wall failure in residential applications. Drainage adds $5-$15 per linear foot but extends wall life from 10-15 years (without drainage) to 50-80+ years (with drainage).

faqs.question: How much does a 30-foot retaining wall cost?
faqs.answer: A 30-foot poured concrete retaining wall at 3-4 feet tall runs $1,200-$2,850 installed in 2026 — typically $1,500-$2,250 for a non-engineered wall with standard drainage. At 5-6 feet tall (engineered), the same 30-foot run jumps to $2,250-$4,500. Add $500-$2,500 for the engineering design stamp.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit for a retaining wall?
faqs.answer: Most jurisdictions require a permit for retaining walls over 4 feet tall (some require it at 3 feet). Walls supporting a surcharge load (driveway, building, pool) typically require a permit regardless of height. Permits run $50-$400 plus the engineering review fee. Where retaining walls cross property lines or affect drainage to neighboring properties, additional municipal review may apply.

faqs.question: How long does a concrete retaining wall last?
faqs.answer: A properly designed and drained concrete retaining wall lasts 50-100 years before requiring structural repair or replacement. Per the Portland Cement Association, residential walls built to current ACI standards with proper drainage and reinforcement have a design life equivalent to a house foundation. The most common failure mode in walls under 20 years old is drainage failure (clogged drain tile) leading to hydrostatic pressure and wall rotation.

faqs.question: What thickness does a concrete retaining wall need?
faqs.answer: Typical residential poured concrete retaining wall thickness: 8 inches for walls up to 4 feet tall, 10 inches for 4-6 feet, 12 inches for 6-8 feet. Engineered walls over 8 feet may require thicker walls plus internal buttresses. Wall thickness is specified by the engineer for engineered walls; for unengineered walls under 4 feet, the local code office can confirm minimum thickness based on the soil and surcharge conditions.

howToSteps.name: Site survey and design
howToSteps.text: Mark the wall location with stakes and string. Survey existing grade. For walls over 4 feet, an engineer designs the wall thickness, footing dimensions, and rebar schedule based on soil class and surcharge load.

howToSteps.name: Excavate the footing trench
howToSteps.text: Excavate the footing trench below the frost line per local code. Width is typically 2x wall thickness; depth depends on wall height and soil class. The trench is graded level and the subgrade compacted.

howToSteps.name: Form and pour the footing
howToSteps.text: Set forms for the footing. Place horizontal rebar (typically two #4 or #5 bars continuous) and vertical rebar dowels that will tie into the wall. Pour concrete (3,500-4,000 PSI) and screed level. Cure 48 hours before forming the wall.

howToSteps.name: Form the wall
howToSteps.text: Set wall forms on top of the cured footing. Forms are braced heavily to resist the lateral pressure of fresh concrete. Place vertical and horizontal rebar per the engineer's spec, tying to the footing dowels for structural continuity.

howToSteps.name: Pour the wall in lifts
howToSteps.text: Pour concrete in 3-4 foot lifts, vibrating each lift to consolidate. The full wall is typically poured in one continuous operation to avoid cold joints. Pour rate must be matched to form capacity to avoid form blowout.

howToSteps.name: Strip forms and cure
howToSteps.text: Strip forms after 48-72 hours. The wall cures for 7 days minimum before backfill is placed. A curing compound is sprayed or wet-cure plastic is laid to maintain moisture.

howToSteps.name: Apply waterproofing and drainage
howToSteps.text: The back face of the wall (the side that will be against soil) receives a waterproofing membrane. A perforated drain tile is set at the footing level in a gravel envelope, routed to daylight or a sump.

howToSteps.name: Backfill in lifts
howToSteps.text: Approved backfill (typically free-draining gravel against the wall, then native soil) is placed in 6-12 inch lifts and compacted. The first 12-24 inches against the wall should always be free-draining material, not native clay or topsoil.

content:
Quick Answer: A poured concrete retaining wall costs $30-$95 per linear foot in 2026 — typically $40-$75 per foot at 3-4 feet tall. CMU block walls run $25-$70 per linear foot at the same height. Walls over 4 feet require engineered design and footings, pushing cost to $75-$150+ per linear foot. Drainage is the single most important line item — adds $5-$15 per foot, extends wall life 4-5x.

Retaining walls have the widest cost range of any residential concrete category because they span from 24-inch cosmetic garden walls to 8-foot engineered structural walls — same name, dramatically different scope. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 retaining wall pricing per linear foot by height, poured vs block, drainage requirements, and when an engineered design is mandatory.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed retaining wall, foundation, slab, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a poured concrete retaining wall runs $30-$95 per linear foot installed nationally, with $40-$75 per foot the most common quote band at 3-4 feet tall. Concrete masonry unit (CMU) block walls run $25-$70 per linear foot at the same height. Wall height is the single largest cost driver: per the International Code Council IRC R404, walls over 4 feet tall require engineered design and footings sized to the soil class, which roughly doubles the per-foot cost vs unengineered 3-foot walls. Drainage system (perforated drain tile plus gravel backfill), wall reinforcement (vertical rebar tied to footing), and excavation depth account for most of the remaining variation between low and high quotes.

2026 pricing by wall height and type

National typical installed pricing per linear foot. Includes excavation, footing, wall, drainage, and standard backfill. Excludes engineering fees on walls over 4 feet, premium finishes, and unusual site access conditions.

Wall heightPoured concreteCMU blockSegmental retaining wall (SRW)Engineering required?
2 ft$25-$45$20-$35$15-$30No
3 ft$30-$60$25-$50$20-$40No (typical)
4 ft$40-$75$35-$60$30-$55Often required
5 ft$55-$95$50-$85$45-$75Yes
6 ft$75-$130$70-$115$60-$100Yes
8 ft$100-$180$90-$160$85-$145Yes + soil-specific design

The 4-foot threshold is the single biggest jump in the table. Below 4 feet, walls are typically built to a standard residential detail without engineering. At 4 feet and above, IRC R404 triggers engineered design, soil-class-specific footing sizing, and more rebar — all of which add $15-$40 per linear foot.

Poured concrete vs CMU block vs segmental retaining wall

Three retaining wall types dominate residential. Each has a cost, lifespan, and best-fit profile.

Poured concrete

Cast-in-place reinforced concrete wall on a concrete footing. Highest per-foot cost, longest lifespan (50-100 years), highest water resistance (no joints), and the only practical option for engineered walls over 8 feet. Installation: 1-2 days for forming and pour, 7 days cure before backfill. Best fit: structural walls, walls under driveways, walls supporting buildings, anywhere water resistance is critical.

CMU block

Concrete masonry units (block) stacked with mortar and grouted vertically with rebar reinforcement on a concrete footing. 15-30 percent cheaper per foot than poured at residential heights, slightly shorter lifespan (40-80 years), and slower install (3-5 days for typical residential wall). Best fit: 3-6 foot residential walls where cost is the priority over speed.

Segmental retaining wall (SRW)

Engineered modular blocks (Versa-Lok, Allan Block, Keystone, etc.) stacked without mortar, often with geogrid soil reinforcement extending behind the wall. Cheapest per-foot at typical residential heights, fastest install (1-3 days for residential), and well-suited to terraced or curved layouts. Lifespan: 30-50+ years depending on block grade and drainage. Best fit: cosmetic and lifestyle retaining walls under 6 feet, especially curved layouts and stepped terraces.

Per the Portland Cement Association, segmental retaining wall systems have grown to over 35 percent of the residential retaining wall market in the U.S. as of 2024, displacing some traditional poured and block work on lifestyle walls under 6 feet.

Why wall height drives cost so much

The per-foot rate climbs nonlinearly with wall height for three reasons.

Footing depth and width

Footing dimensions scale with wall height and the load they resist. A 3-foot wall typically has a 12-inch wide, 8-inch deep footing. A 6-foot wall typically has a 24-30 inch wide, 12-inch deep footing — roughly 4x the concrete volume per linear foot.

Reinforcement

Vertical rebar in the wall and horizontal rebar in the footing scale with wall height. A 3-foot wall typically has #4 rebar at 24-inch centers. A 6-foot wall typically has #5 rebar at 12-inch centers — roughly 4x the rebar weight per linear foot.

Engineering and permitting

Engineered design adds $500-$2,500 to project cost. On a short 25-foot wall, that's $20-$100 per linear foot of engineering alone. Permits add another $50-$400.

Drainage: the most important line item

Per the Federal Highway Administration, inadequate drainage is the leading cause of retaining wall failure in residential applications. A wall without proper drainage holds water behind it; water pressure (hydrostatic) builds up; the wall rotates, cracks, or fails entirely.

A proper drainage system includes three components:

  • Perforated drain tile (4-inch PVC) at the back of the footing, routed to daylight or a sump
  • Free-draining gravel envelope around the drain tile, extending up against the wall
  • Filter fabric between the gravel and native soil to prevent fine soil from clogging the drain

Total cost: $5-$15 per linear foot of wall. Skipping it saves a few hundred dollars upfront and costs $5,000-$30,000+ in wall repair or replacement within 10-15 years. According to industry data tracked by the American Concrete Institute, retaining walls with proper drainage have roughly 5-8x the service life of walls without it.

How a poured concrete retaining wall is installed

  1. Site survey and design. Mark the wall location with stakes and string. Survey existing grade. For walls over 4 feet, an engineer designs the wall thickness, footing dimensions, and rebar schedule based on soil class and surcharge load.
  2. Excavate the footing trench. Excavate the footing trench below the frost line per local code. Width is typically 2x wall thickness; depth depends on wall height and soil class. The trench is graded level and the subgrade compacted.
  3. Form and pour the footing. Set forms for the footing. Place horizontal rebar (typically two #4 or #5 bars continuous) and vertical rebar dowels that will tie into the wall. Pour concrete (3,500-4,000 PSI) and screed level. Cure 48 hours before forming the wall.
  4. Form the wall. Set wall forms on top of the cured footing. Forms are braced heavily to resist the lateral pressure of fresh concrete. Place vertical and horizontal rebar per the engineer's spec, tying to the footing dowels for structural continuity.
  5. Pour the wall in lifts. Pour concrete in 3-4 foot lifts, vibrating each lift to consolidate. The full wall is typically poured in one continuous operation to avoid cold joints. Pour rate must be matched to form capacity to avoid form blowout.
  6. Strip forms and cure. Strip forms after 48-72 hours. The wall cures for 7 days minimum before backfill is placed. A curing compound is sprayed or wet-cure plastic is laid to maintain moisture.
  7. Apply waterproofing and drainage. The back face of the wall (the side that will be against soil) receives a waterproofing membrane. A perforated drain tile is set at the footing level in a gravel envelope, routed to daylight or a sump.
  8. Backfill in lifts. Approved backfill (typically free-draining gravel against the wall, then native soil) is placed in 6-12 inch lifts and compacted. The first 12-24 inches against the wall should always be free-draining material, not native clay or topsoil.

Common cost drivers beyond height

Site access

If a concrete truck or pump cannot get within 50-100 feet of the wall location, pumping fees ($350-$1,200 per pour) or wheelbarrow-from-truck labor adds $5-$20 per linear foot. Backyard walls behind houses with narrow side access are the most common site-access cost driver.

Soil class

Expansive clay, organic soils, and high water tables all require larger footings or specialized backfill, adding $5-$30 per linear foot. A geotechnical report ($1,500-$4,000) is often required for engineered walls over 6 feet.

Finishes

Standard wall is left as poured. Decorative finishes add real cost: form-liner texture (brick, stone, board-form) +$8-$25 per linear foot; integral color +$3-$8 per linear foot; stucco or stone veneer +$15-$40 per linear foot. For comparison with other decorative concrete options, see Cost of Concrete's stamped concrete cost guide.

Length

Per-foot rates trend down on longer walls because mobilization and form costs amortize. Walls under 20 linear feet often carry a $300-$800 small-job surcharge.

Regional pricing variation

Retaining wall pricing varies 25-40 percent between U.S. metros. Highest pricing: San Francisco Bay, Seattle, Honolulu, Boston, New York at $55-$130 per linear foot at 3-4 feet tall. Lowest: South-Central U.S. (Texas, Oklahoma, Tennessee) at $30-$55 per linear foot at the same height. For full foundation-and-structural concrete context, see Cost of Concrete's concrete foundation cost guide.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a concrete retaining wall cost per linear foot in 2026?

A poured concrete retaining wall runs $30-$95 per linear foot in 2026, with $40-$75 per foot the most common band at 3-4 feet tall. CMU block walls at the same height run $25-$70 per linear foot. Walls under 3 feet tall price toward the low end; engineered walls over 4 feet jump to $75-$150+ per foot because of structural design, deeper footing, and heavier reinforcement requirements.

What height retaining wall requires an engineered design?

Per the International Code Council IRC R404 (residential code), retaining walls over 4 feet tall measured from the bottom of the footing to the top of the wall require an engineered design stamped by a licensed structural engineer. Some jurisdictions trigger the engineering requirement at 3 feet for walls supporting a surcharge load (driveway, structure, vehicle). Engineering fees add $500-$2,500 to project cost.

Is poured concrete cheaper than block for a retaining wall?

Block (CMU) is typically 15-30 percent cheaper than poured concrete on a wall-only basis at residential heights. Poured concrete wins on speed of installation (1-2 days vs 3-5 days for block), water resistance (no mortar joints to fail), and structural integrity for taller walls. Most engineered walls over 6 feet are poured concrete; most short cosmetic walls are block or segmental retaining wall (SRW) units.

Do retaining walls need drainage?

Yes — drainage is the single highest-leverage line item in retaining wall design. A perforated drain tile in a gravel envelope at the back of the footing, draining to daylight or a sump, prevents hydrostatic pressure buildup behind the wall. The Federal Highway Administration cites inadequate drainage as the leading cause of retaining wall failure in residential applications. Drainage adds $5-$15 per linear foot but extends wall life from 10-15 years (without drainage) to 50-80+ years (with drainage).

How much does a 30-foot retaining wall cost?

A 30-foot poured concrete retaining wall at 3-4 feet tall runs $1,200-$2,850 installed in 2026 — typically $1,500-$2,250 for a non-engineered wall with standard drainage. At 5-6 feet tall (engineered), the same 30-foot run jumps to $2,250-$4,500. Add $500-$2,500 for the engineering design stamp.

Do I need a permit for a retaining wall?

Most jurisdictions require a permit for retaining walls over 4 feet tall (some require it at 3 feet). Walls supporting a surcharge load (driveway, building, pool) typically require a permit regardless of height. Permits run $50-$400 plus the engineering review fee. Where retaining walls cross property lines or affect drainage to neighboring properties, additional municipal review may apply.

How long does a concrete retaining wall last?

A properly designed and drained concrete retaining wall lasts 50-100 years before requiring structural repair or replacement. Per the Portland Cement Association, residential walls built to current ACI standards with proper drainage and reinforcement have a design life equivalent to a house foundation. The most common failure mode in walls under 20 years old is drainage failure (clogged drain tile) leading to hydrostatic pressure and wall rotation.

What thickness does a concrete retaining wall need?

Typical residential poured concrete retaining wall thickness: 8 inches for walls up to 4 feet tall, 10 inches for 4-6 feet, 12 inches for 6-8 feet. Engineered walls over 8 feet may require thicker walls plus internal buttresses. Wall thickness is specified by the engineer for engineered walls; for unengineered walls under 4 feet, the local code office can confirm minimum thickness based on the soil and surcharge conditions.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 national pricing: poured concrete $30-$95 per linear foot; CMU block $25-$70 per linear foot at 3-4 feet tall.
  • The 4-foot threshold is the single biggest cost jump — walls over 4 feet require engineered design, deeper footings, heavier rebar, adding $15-$40 per linear foot plus engineering fees.
  • Drainage (perforated drain tile + gravel envelope + filter fabric) adds $5-$15 per linear foot and extends wall life 4-5x. Skipping it is the single biggest mistake on a residential wall.
  • SRW (segmental retaining wall) systems have captured 35+ percent of the residential market under 6 feet because of speed and aesthetic flexibility.
  • Engineered designs require a stamped drawing ($500-$2,500) plus a building permit ($50-$400).
  • Wall thickness scales with height: 8 inches for ≤4ft, 10 inches for 4-6ft, 12 inches for 6-8ft.

Need a contractor whose retaining wall quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local retaining wall pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
